figures.caption: Fig. 4: Confusion matrix for action classification. While SCOTTI achieves high accuracy overall, misclassifications are observed between actions with similar tactile signal patterns. In particular, step-up exercises are sometimes misclassified as SideWalking or Lunge, and conversely, SideWalking samples are often confused with step-up exercises.

Fig. 2: Qualitative result of pose estimation and progress prediction results for lunges (left) and squats (right). The results demonstrate SCOTTI’s ability to accurately predict both pose and progress for different activities.
TABLE I: Comparison of SCOTTI with baselines. Arrows indicate the desired direction for each metric, and bold numbers indicate the best performance.
TABLE II: Comparison of SCOTTI with multi-task learning versions of baselines. Arrows indicate the desired direction for each metric, and bold numbers indicate the best performance.

Fig. 5: Analysis of SCOTTI. (a) Importance of different foot regions for SCOTTI across tasks, highlighting the central foot regions’ significance. (b) t-SNE visualization of shared features based on progress values, showing structured patterns for different actions. (c) t-SNE visualization of shared features based on action labels, demonstrating well-clustered features for individual actions.
